# Shiv Misra Joins Marriott International to Lead Data, AI & Emerging Technologies
[...]
Shiv Misra, Marriott International Managing Vice President, Data, AI & Emerging Technologies
[...]
Marriott International has appointed Shiv Misra as Managing Vice President, Data, AI & Emerging Technologies, where he steps into a global technology leadership role overseeing the company’s enterprise data, analytics, AI, and emerging technology initiatives.
[...]
In this role, Misra will support enterprise modernization efforts and help advance next-generation technology capabilities across the global hospitality company’s customers, associates, and business operations. His responsibilities include driving innovation and operational impact through data platforms, AI solutions, and personalization capabilities, while also supporting emerging technology initiatives spanning areas such as Quantum Computing, Internet of Things (IoT), sensor-based technologies, and Digital Twins.
[...]
Prior to joining Marriott, Misra led enterprise AI, data, and technology transformation initiatives across Fortune 500 organizations spanning healthcare, hospitality, consumer goods, financial services, media, and technology. Most recently at McKesson, he architected the company’s enterprise AI strategy and modernization agenda from the ground up, establishing the operating models, governance, platforms, and scalable capabilities required to embed AI across the enterprise while helping unlock billions of dollars in incremental business impact.
[...]
Known for operating at the intersection of technology strategy and execution, Misra has built global AI and analytics organizations and led enterprise technology ecosystems designed to translate emerging technologies into measurable business outcomes.
[...]
Reflecting on the transition, Misra shared, “Returning to hospitality in a global technology leadership capacity feels deeply meaningful. I’m excited by the opportunity to help shape the future of data, AI, and emerging technologies at Marriott while building capabilities that strengthen innovation, elevate experiences, and create long-term enterprise value.” Shiv Misra holds a position on the CDO Magazine Global Board, showcasing his contributions to the field of data. He has been recognized on several industry lists, including Top 30 Healthcare Innovators 2026, 40 Under 40 Data Leaders 2025, and Data Leaders 100 2025.

results.title: Marriott's AI Rollout: From Pilots to ‘Points on the Board’
highlights:
# Marriott's AI Rollout: From Pilots to ‘Points on the Board’
[...]
Marriott's AI Rollout: From Pilots to ‘Points on the Board’
[...]
Marriott is now in the third phase of its AI rollout, with conversational search the highest-profile launch coming this year. Its top data and AI exec says the harder work is rethinking the workflows underneath.
[...]
Marriott is in the third phase of its AI rollout, moving past pilots and operating-system builds and starting to deliver on revenue and cost-saving solutions.
[...]
Marriott segments its AI work into three tiers, according to Colin Coleman, SVP of Enterprise Data, Analytics, and AI at Marriott International. There is Microsoft Copilot rolled out to nearly every employee, low-code and no-code tools at the team level, and what he called “industrial-strength solutions” at the top, focused on driving revenue or cost savings.
[...]
“Industrial-strength solutions now are actually putting the points on the board,” said Coleman at the Skift Data + AI Summit on Wednesday.
[...]
He also described the path to get to this point. 2024 was the “year of pilots.” The next phase focused on building the enterprise operating system and the “horizontal intelligence layers” needed to support AI at scale. Marriott started on the cost side — contact center support, marketing, on-property use cases — before turning to consumer-facing AI.
[...]
## Conversational Search
[...]
Marriott plans to launch conversational search on its website by the end of Q2, which brings different challenges than the AI tools used for reducing costs.
[...]
Marriott isn’t starting from scratch. Drew Pinto, chief revenue and technology officer, said in March that the company has run natural-language search on its Homes & Villas by Marriott Bonvoy site for about two years.
[...]
## Beyond Automation
[...]
Coleman is pushing the company to redesign workflows before automating them, framing automation alone as a missed opportunity in how AI is rolled out.
[...]
“Let’s not talk about automation, let’s talk about how you really sit with that end stakeholder working in a hotel, looking at what they’re doing, and then how do you re-engineer what they’re doing,” he said.
[...]
He offered the example of a hotel revenue manager surrounded by spreadsheets. “The number of Excel worksheets that are flying around, the manual processes — if all you did was just automate what they’re currently doing, you might be missing the boat, because they’re doing what they’re doing because they have to do it that way,” he said.
[...]
Coleman, who joined Marriott just over a year ago from outside the hotel industry, offered a contrarian observation for an executive in his role.
[...]
“My observation coming into hospitality was there were enough gains on the table already to be had if Gen AI wasn’t even in the room,” he said.
[...]
Gains were available from connecting customer data and operations data with or without generative AI, he said.
[...]
“The build part is actually the easiest thing to crack,” he said. “It’s the skilling of the workforce to have these discussions about what are the right ways to solve those problems. The team implications are huge across the board.”

results.title: Marriott advances AI rollout toward revenue outcomes | Let's Data Science
highlights:
Marriott advances AI rollout toward revenue outcomes | Let's Data Science

Feedback

Photo: skift.com· rights & takedowns

Quick SummaryHide

Marriott International has entered the third phase of its AI rollout, moving beyond pilots and platform builds toward implementations meant to deliver measurable revenue and cost savings, Skift reported from its Data + AI Summit. Colin Coleman, Marriott's senior vice president of enterprise data, analytics, and AI, described a three-tier approach: Microsoft Copilot deployed to nearly every employee, low-code and no-code tools at the team level, and 'industrial-strength solutions' aimed at driving revenue or reducing costs, per Skift. Coleman identified conversational search, building on the company's Homes and Villas platform, as Marriott's highest-profile AI launch this year, and said connecting customer and operations data can produce gains even without generative AI. 'Industrial-strength solutions now are actually putting the points on the board,' he said, per Skift. The remarks illustrate how large enterprises are shifting AI from experimentation toward production systems tied to business outcomes.

### What happened

Marriott International has entered the third phase of its AI rollout, moving past pilots and platform builds toward implementations that deliver revenue and cost savings, Skift reported from its Data + AI Summit.

### The three-tier approach

Colin Coleman, Marriott's senior vice president of enterprise data, analytics, and AI, described three tiers of AI work, per Skift: Microsoft Copilot rolled out to nearly every employee, low-code and no-code tools at the team level, and top-tier 'industrial-strength solutions' focused on revenue or cost outcomes. Coleman said connecting customer and operations data can produce gains even without generative AI.

### Conversational search

Coleman identified conversational search, building on Marriott's Homes and Villas platform, as the company's highest-profile AI launch this year, per Skift. He emphasized redesigning underlying workflows before automating them. 'Industrial-strength solutions now are actually putting the points on the board,' he said, per Skift.

### Why it matters (analysis)

Generic-industry experience shows enterprises often run a similar three-layer AI stack: broad assistants, team-level low-code tooling, and a few high-impact production projects. Integrating customer and operations data frequently yields measurable results even without generative-model novelty. For practitioners, the move from pilots to production tied to revenue and cost metrics is a common marker of AI maturity.

## Scoring Rationale

A substantive enterprise-AI deployment case study from a major hospitality brand, detailing a three-tier strategy and a shift from pilots to production tied to revenue and cost metrics. Useful and concrete for practitioners tracking enterprise adoption, though it is a single-company vertical deployment rather than a broad industry event. Scored in the notable range.

results.title: The Data Architecture Gap That Hotel AI Procurement Isn’t Pricing In - wellnessfitpro
highlights:
Colin Coleman, Marriott’s Senior Vice President of Enterprise Data, Analytics and AI, is running that build across 9,900 properties and more than 30 brands, and his central argument at Skift Data + AI Summit on June 3 in New York is architectural rather than aspirational. The advantage, Coleman argues, is not the volume of data — 283 million Bonvoy members, decades of first-party signals spanning the full guest journey — but the connected intelligence layer Marriott is constructing on top of it, a foundation that makes every new AI application deployed across the portfolio start smarter than the last one rather than starting from scratch.
[...]
Every GenAI tool inside Marriott draws from the same connected layer linking customer, property, and owner data, which means the second tool is better than the first and the tenth is dramatically better than the second.
[...]
The implication for the rest of the industry is uncomfortable and worth sitting with. Hotel companies buying AI capability in 2026 without building the data services layer underneath it are committing to tools that will underperform and eventually need replacing. Coleman is making that argument from inside the build, which gives it credibility but also raises the question the room needs to press him on directly.
[...]
for anyone evaluating hotel technology investments right now.
[...]
The first is the intelligence layer versus the point solution — most hotel AI investment gets evaluated deal by deal, does this pricing tool improve RevPAR, does this chatbot reduce call volume, does this personalization engine lift conversion, and the question Coleman is pushing is whether those tools compound or whether each deployment starts from zero and the cost of integrating the next one keeps rising. Companies treating each AI buy as an isolated decision are accumulating technical debt that doesn’t appear on the procurement spreadsheet.
[...]
## Two architectures for hotel AI procurement: the point solution model versus the intelligence layer model, comparing integration cost curves and outcomes.
[...]
What Marriott is building
[...]
Intelligence Layer Model
[...]
Central data layer connects customer, property, and owner signals. One foundation serves every AI application.
[...]
Every new tool starts smarter than the last. Connected context means compounding intelligence across deployments.
[...]
Integration cost decreases with each addition. The shared layer absorbs complexity individual tools don’t have to carry.
[...]
4

Human-AI design constraint built in. Organizational infrastructure — literacy, decision rights, champion networks — runs alongside the technical build.
[...]
The second is the human-AI design constraint — Coleman’s operating principle is that AI earns its place at Marriott by enabling associates to spend less time on repetitive tasks and more time on guest experience, which means organizational infrastructure running alongside the technical infrastructure, AI literacy programs for on-property associates, decision-rights frameworks clarifying what AI can and cannot act on alone.
[...]
Here is the tension Skift will press directly. Marriott’s data advantage is 283 million loyalty members and a Google Cloud partnership to lean on. Most hotel companies don’t have that. Independent operators, regional chains, mid-scale brands — they are working with a fraction of the data and a fraction of the engineering resources, and if the lesson from Marriott’s AI build is “start with your data foundation,” the honest follow-up question is: what does that mean for the operator who doesn’t have one? Is Coleman’s framework a roadmap that scales to a 50-property portfolio, or is it a description of a moat that only looks like advice?
[...]
Coleman joins Joff Romoff, Global Head of Travel and Hospitality at Google Cloud, with Seth Borko, Skift’s Head of Research, moderating. A limited number of seats remain for Skift Data + AI Summit 2026.
